one_line_takeaway: A vision paper arguing LLM-agent tool-use safety should shift from probabilistic model-based guards to STPA-derived, formally enforceable specifications backed by a capability-enhanced MCP with mandatory confidentiality/capability/trust labels, demonstrated only via a small Alloy formal model of a calendar agent.
key_finding: Feasibility demonstration only: in the Alloy model, without policies the Alloy Analyzer quickly finds counterexamples to the safety specs (e.g., private data leaking into an external email); with the policies and sanitation steps enforced, the analyzer's bounded exhaustive trace search confirms the hazardous predicates cannot be satisfied while safe traces still remain (create event, reschedule, email participants without leaking private data). The authors read this as evidence that unsafe flows can be deterministically blocked without collapsing the agent's useful capability, and that autonomy can be a configurable design choice where users are prompted only when a decision could cause an actual loss rather than on every tool call.
core_contribution: A vision/NIER paper proposing a process to move LLM-agent tool-use safety from probabilistic safeguards to deterministic guardrails with formal guarantees. The authors' OWN contributions are threefold: (1) adapting System-Theoretic Process Analysis (STPA) to agent workflows to systematically identify hazards, derive stakeholder values/losses, and produce safety/security requirements; (2) refining those requirements into symbolic, enforceable specifications (an information-flow constraint and a temporal-logic constraint in their running example) enforced via a four-tier structure of Blocklist / Mustlist / Allowlist / Confirmation applied at tool boundaries by an entity independent of the agent; and (3) a capability-enhanced Model Context Protocol (MCP) extension that replaces MCP's optional annotation hints with REQUIRED structured key-value labels (minimally: Capabilities, Data Confidentiality, Trust Level) so that IFC/temporal policies can actually be enforced at runtime. Cited systems (GuardAgent, ShieldAgent, TrustAgent, Agent-as-a-Judge, RTBAS, IsolateGPT, etc.) are surveyed as prior/related work, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Task-specific LLM agents that invoke tools (calendars, email, APIs, databases, code sandboxes, other agents) can produce unsafe outcomes from unconstrained tool composition, whether from malicious inputs (e.g., indirect prompt injection such as the cited GitHub MCP exploit where injected instructions made an agent read sensitive repo files then publish them in a public commit/PR) OR from ordinary model errors (no attacker required). Concrete harms motivating the work: leaking sensitive data, overwriting or deleting critical records, mistakenly rescheduling high-priority meetings. Setting is enterprise / high-assurance (customer data, medical records) where even low-probability unsafe flows are unacceptable. The authors explicitly argue model-based judges and pattern filters only raise detection probability and give no guarantee, since a persistent attacker needs only one success and can tailor attacks to a specific defense; they deliberately scope to narrow task-specific agents because general-purpose agents are hard to specify precisely.
limitations: This is an early-stage NIER vision paper: evidence is a single hand-built Alloy model of one 3-tool calendar agent, not an implemented system or empirical evaluation. Alloy's bounded analysis guarantees only up to a finite scope, not unbounded soundness. No policy engine has been built yet (explicitly future work), no measurement of coverage, usability, notification fatigue, or the safety/utility tradeoff exists. The whole approach hinges on obtaining trustworthy structured labels: the authors concede labels are costly, that MCP advises treating tools as untrusted, and that for open tool markets the required label metadata cannot be trusted without additional (unspecified) certification/verification mechanisms. Approach is deliberately scoped to narrow task-specific agents and explicitly does not solve general-purpose agent safety. STPA hazard identification and requirement-to-spec refinement are manual, expert-driven steps whose scalability is unproven.

quantitative_results: No benchmark metrics reported; this is a 5-page ICSE-NIER vision paper with no experimental evaluation

Sole formal evidence: an Alloy model of one calendar agent with 3 tools (list_events, update_event, send_email); Alloy Analyzer finds safety counterexamples without policies and finds no hazardous-predicate satisfaction (while safe traces remain) once Blocklist/Mustlist/Allowlist/Confirmation policies and sanitation steps are enforced
Proposes minimally 3 required MCP label categories: Capabilities, Data Confidentiality, Trust Level
baselines_compared: No empirical baseline comparison. Prior approaches are only discussed/critiqued qualitatively as related work: human-in-the-loop confirmation (habituation/security fatigue), model-based judges and pattern-matching filters (GuardAgent, ShieldAgent, TrustAgent, Agent-as-a-Judge; no guarantees), prompt-injection detectors (Attention Tracker, PromptShield), and IFC/isolation work (Costa et al. IFC, Siddiqui et al. permissive IFC, RTBAS, IsolateGPT, CaMeL/Defeating Prompt Injections by Design)
